summary.title: TicTacDoh function-level threat model
summary.description: Local two-player Python tkinter desktop application. SQLite-backed user accounts, JSON-backed saved games. Two players share the same physical PC and same Python process. No network, no server.

data.name: Player 1
data.description: Local user, host PC. Starts the program.

data.name: Player 2 (opponent)
data.description: Opponent on the same physical PC. Enters own credentials.

threats.title: Spoofing: account creation under any name
threats.description: Player 1 can create accounts under any chosen username with no identity proof. Combined with later DB read, the attacker controls a known credential pair.

threats.title: Information Disclosure: cleartext password crosses TB-1
threats.description: Password travels in cleartext from the keyboard into the process before being hashed by P2.

threats.title: Information Disclosure: cleartext in process memory
threats.description: Cleartext password sits briefly in process memory before hashing. Local attacker with debugger access only.

threats.title: Spoofing: brute-force login unrestricted
threats.description: No rate-limit on the login dialog. A held-down key or script can run brute-force credential attempts indefinitely.

threats.title: Denial of Service: login dialog floodable
threats.description: Continuous rapid login attempts can render the GUI unresponsive.

data.name: TB1
data.description: User to App boundary. Separates external entities (keyboard input) from the running Python process.

data.name: TB2
data.description: App to Filesystem boundary. Separates the running process from the local filesystem.

threats.title: Information Disclosure: cleartext password in memory
threats.description: Candidate password lives in process memory during verify. Local risk only.

data.name: DF5
data.description: INSERT user (MD5 hash) — written to SQLite

threats.title: Tampering: any local user can rewrite hash rows
threats.description: tictactoe.db is world-readable and writable. Anyone with file access can replace a user's hash and log in as them.

threats.title: Information Disclosure: MD5 hashes crackable offline
threats.description: Once the DB is read, hashcat -m 0 recovers passwords in seconds on a single GPU.

threats.title: Information Disclosure: hash crosses TB-2
threats.description: Password hash is read from the filesystem back into the process on every login attempt.

threats.title: Information Disclosure: anonymous account enumeration
threats.description: Scoreboard is shown to unauthenticated users from the main menu, leaking the existence and activity of every account.

data.name: DF8
data.description: Player 2 cleartext password handed to Player 1's session — design flaw, Issue #3

threats.title: Spoofing: credential disclosed to third party
threats.description: Player 2 types their cleartext password into a dialog owned by Player 1's already-authenticated process. Player 1 can install a keylogger, swap the dialog, or read the value via a debugger.

threats.title: Tampering: dialog can be replaced
threats.description: The askstring dialog runs in Player 1's process. Nothing prevents Player 1 from replacing it with a fake form before Player 2 types.

data.name: TB3
data.description: Authentication boundary. self.current_user is set once on login and never re-checked. Anonymous below, authenticated above (or vice versa depending on layout).

threats.title: Spoofing: password handed back across auth boundary
threats.description: Post-auth process P5 forwards Player 2's cleartext password back into pre-auth process P3. Player 1's session can intercept or replace the value before forwarding.

data.name: DS1
data.description: tictactoe.db (SQLite, world-readable). Holds users, MD5 hashes, games table.

data.name: DS3
data.description: GUI / messagebox. tkinter feedback surface. Reveals game_id on save.

data.name: DS2
data.description: savegames/*.json. Plain-text, no signature, tamperable, unbounded growth.
